paragraphs.source_preview: Wafer Demand Model, Node Economics, and the shifting power dynamics as AI reshapes the foundry landscape
By Sravan Kundojjala, Gerald Wong, Dylan Patel, and Jeff Koch
Jan 08, 2026 · Paid
In 2013, TSMC made a $10 billion bet on a single customer. Morris Chang committed to building 20nm capacity with uncertain economics on the promise that Apple w
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!PHO_!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Apple’s annual spend at TSMC grew from $2B in 2014 to $24B in 2025. That is 12x in 12 years. Apple went from 9% of TSMC revenue to 25% at its peak and settled t
The foundry model is dominant today. IDMs cannot support process development and fab capex with what is effectively a single customer. But even foundries need a
This report traces the Apple-TSMC relationship from Intel’s 2010 rejection through five distinct phases, examining how Apple built its chip empire through acqui
You can see the Apple effect in TSMC’s capex trajectory. Pre-Apple (2005-2009), annual spending averaged $2.4B with no anchor tenant to de-risk investment. From
The platform shift has already happened. HPC revenue at TSMC grew from 36% in 2020 to 58% in 2025. Smartphone revenue shrunk to 29% from 46%. Our model shows Nv
Consensus sees this as permanent displacement. Our model says otherwise.
TSMC’s A16 is architected for HPC. Backside power delivery, gate-all-around transistors, and thermal envelopes are optimized for HPC and smartphones will skip t
Apple is not standing still. New chip families (N-series, C-series) account for 15% of wafer demand by 2030. iPhone’s share of Apple’s wafer mix fell from 74% t
What This Analysis Covers
The analysis is built on two proprietary models. Our [Foundry Model](https://semianalysis.com/foundry-industry-model/) tracks revenue, wafer shipments, and capa
The report concludes with Taiwan risk scenarios, Arizona economics, and conditions under which Apple would build its own fabs.
Wafer Demand
· M-series: $0 (2019) to $4.9B (2025) = From zero to $5B in 6 years
· S-series: $86M (2018) to $342M (2025) = 4x growth
· Total Apple Silicon Revenue: $23.5B (2025)
TSMC Transformation:
· Revenue: $13B (2010) to $122B (2025) = 9.4x growth
· Gross Margin: 45.5% (2010) to 59%+ (2025) = 13.5pp expansion
The Packaging Revolution:
· CoWoS Revenue: $0.6B (2018) to $8.4B (2025) = 14x growth
· Apple InFO Revenue: $3.5B+ (2025)
· TSMC Advanced Packaging as % of Revenue: 6-7% (2018) to 10%+ (2025)
Apple’s Supply Chain Leverage:
· Manufacturing Purchase Obligations: $8.7B (2010) to $56.2B (2025) = 6.4x growth
· Monthly Wafer Demand: 19K (2013) to 130k (2025) = 7x growth
· Fab 18 Customers: 4 (2020) to 45 (2025) = 11x growth
Chip Economics:
· iPhone Gross Margin: 36.5% (A4, 2018) to 41.5% (A18, 2025) = 5pp expansion
· Mac Gross Margin: 29% (Intel, 2019) to 39.5% (M3 Pro, 2023) = 10.5pp expansion
· Annual Chip Savings: $7B+ (Intel $5B + Qualcomm $1.2B + Broadcom $700M + custom IP $500M+)
Platform Shift at TSMC:
· Smartphone Revenue %: 46% (Q1 2028) to 29% (Q4 2025)
· HPC Revenue %: 36% (Q1 2020) to 58% (Q4 2025) = HPC now dominates

sections.title: The Five Phases of Apple-TSMC Evolution
sections.title_translation: 苹果与台积电合作的五个阶段

paragraphs.source_preview: https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!0uZo!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Phase 1: Why Apple started making its own chips and Apple’s courtship
The original iPhone launched in 2007 relied heavily on Samsung components. Samsung supplied the application processor, display, and flash memory. While making i
First, Apple’s key supplier Samsung entered smartphones 18 months after iPhone launched, ending up in a legal battle with Apple as early Galaxy S designs resemb
Second, the rise of the Wintel model (Android plus Qualcomm) in smartphones in the 2010s worried Apple that commodity chips could erode its differentiation in s
Third, workload optimization: designing for iOS specifically, rather than generic benchmarks, enabled meaningful performance-per-watt advantages.
Fourth, power efficiency: iPhone’s thin form factor demanded performance-per-watt leadership that merchant silicon couldn’t deliver.
Fifth, margins: eliminating supplier markups over time would capture billions in additional profit.
Apple wanted to control the primary technologies used in its devices. The $278M P.A. Semi acquisition in April 2008 served as a stepping stone. Dan Dobberpuhl,

First and foremost, if we do this, can we deliver better products? That’s the No. 1 question. It’s not about the chip. Apple is not a chip company.

- Johny Sro
Following P.A. Semi, Apple acquired ultra-low-power chip design company Intrinsity for $121M in 2010 Apple fielded its first custom smartphone applications proc
The Decision That Changed Computing History: TSMC vs Samsung vs Intel Decision
Between 2010 and 2014, the “courtship” phase, Apple explored alternatives to Samsung via “Project Azalea,” considering GlobalFoundries and even building its own
Enter Intel and TSMC, two of the leading fab options for Apple. Discussions with Intel were unsuccessful as then CEO Paul Otellini declined, believing the volum
Apple COO Jeff Williams met with Chang over dinner and pitched TSMC to build 20nm capacity. At that time, TSMC was shifting focus and investment to 16nm. The ca
Phase 2: Apple Made TSMC (2014-2020)
Apple’s A8 chip launched in 2014, and TSMC never looked back. Over the next six years, Apple drove TSMC to invest $60-80 billion in leading-edge capacity. Apple
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!nT85!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Yiqb!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!VYK-!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
In 2016, Apple funded the development of InFO (Integrated Fan-Out) packaging. This allowed for thinner phones with better thermals and created the advanced pack
In a candid interview, Morris Chang revealed that Tim Cook later told him:
Intel just does not know how to be a foundry.
At the core there are cultural differences: Intel, trapped in its product success, couldn’t foresee the massive Arm-based foundry market; TSMC offered servitude
Apple initially said to have offered 40% gross margin to TSMC, which was in line with TSMC’s margin then. Current gross margin from Apple business is significan
Despite investing in dedicated 20nm capacity for Apple, TSMC did not even receive a majority share of Apple’s foundry business at first. It had to share a 14nm
Ultimately, TSMC won because it demonstrated the ability to scale 20nm ahead of the competition, proving it could handle the iPhone’s massive volume spikes. The
What if Apple chose Intel in 2014? Intel would have $15B+/year guaranteed foundry revenue. TSMC, without that revenue, would probably not have achieved dominanc
Phase 3: Mutual Lock-In (2020-2023)
By 2020, the partnership had evolved from mutually beneficial to co-dependence. Apple could no longer leave. No other foundry on earth could produce M-series an
TSMC could not lose Apple. The iPhone brought 22-25% of total revenue and filled 70%+ of 3nm capacity. Apple orders were known three years in advance, allowing
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!HMKq!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!uSih!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!_7t3!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
A more serious risk is timing. Apple’s product cadence is synchronized to TSMC’s node roadmap. Moving to Intel or Samsung would mean 2-3 years of inferior produ
Phase 4: Diversified Dependence (2023-Present): The Changing Power Dynamic: Is Apple Still the Kingmaker?
For years, Hsinchu and Cupertino worked as a single team to relentlessly advance Moore’s Law. Apple’s One Team approach embedded hundreds of engineers at TSMC’s
However, the rise of generative AI is shifting TSMC’s customer mix. While Apple remains the largest single customer by revenue, its relative influence is diluti
While Apple also contributes to TSMC’s HPC segment through its tablet/PC chips, the uptake of AI offered TSMC a new stream of customers who are voracious for ad
Apple acts as the predictable baseline that justifies the massive, fixed costs of new fabs. NVIDIA provides the high-margin upside that drives profitability gro
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!CVJM!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
The AI Divergence: Wafers vs. Packaging
The distinction lies in what these customers are buying. Apple buys leading-edge logic wafers (N3, N3E) and InFO (Integrated Fan-Out) packaging. NVIDIA buys log
Apple was TSMC’s first advanced packaging customer at scale. InFO revenue grew from $1.8B in 2018 to >$3.5B in 2024, driven entirely by A-series and M-series ch
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!3bJr!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Vy_p!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
This creates a bifurcation in TSMC’s capacity planning. Apple is no longer the sole driver of capex. TSMC’s capital expenditure is now split between chasing Moo
Phase 5: Beyond TSMC (2027+)
Apple is actively exploring alternatives.
Intel’s 18A-P process (shipping late 2026) represents the first theoretically viable alternative since Apple left Samsung in 2016. Apple could qualify Intel, in
Intel missed Apple once before in 2014. The door hasn’t fully closed. The key question is: would Apple actually use it?
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Mkbg!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!3RG5!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
But, 18A-P on base M-series chips makes sense. If Apple were to shift 20% of base M-series wafers to Intel 18A-P, it would imply $630M in foundry revenue for In
Base M-series die sizes tend to be in the range of 150-170mm² and could yield 70%+ on 18A-P (based on current Panther Lake, which has a similar die size).
While Intel’s current 18A yields are lower than TSMC’s N3 for a 150mm² die (>80%), Intel offers pricing leverage, potential 14A optionality, and US-based wafer/
Intel 18A-P offers 8% higher performance/watt and similar density to 18A with backside power delivery (PowerVia).
There are a few lower impact possibilities too: while the Intel16 mature node in its Ireland fab could address DTV and connectivity apps, we doubt the capacity
Apple’s Real Diversification Strategy
Apple’s actual foundry diversification isn’t about moving leading-edge A-series/M-series away from TSMC. Non-Pro versions, Peripheral chips and packaging are al
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!g79U!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Where diversification makes sense: PMICs, Display Drivers, Audio/Connectivity
Where diversification is Challenge: leading-edge A-series and M-series
Apple’s Reengagement with Samsung LSI/Foundry
After leaving Samsung in 2016, Apple went exclusive with TSMC. Apple’s diversification strategy is about reducing supply chain risk for non-critical chips.
Apple has signed a strategic deal with Samsung Foundry to manufacture advanced CMOS Image Sensors (CIS) at Samsung’s Austin, Texas facilities, breaking Sony’s d
We estimate Samsung could capture 20-30% of Apple’s CIS volume by 2027 (150M-200M sensors annually), offering $1-$1.5B foundry revenue to Samsung.
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!zvqD!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Mapping the Apple-TSMC Ecosystem: Where the Silicon is Born
To understand the relationship, one must understand the physical reality of where these chips are made. TSMC’s dispersed network of GigaFabs, each specializing
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!j0vc!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
The Critical Back-End: Advanced Packaging
Advanced packaging is an area where the divergence between Apple and NVIDIA is most acute. Apple relies on InFO-PoP (Integrated Fan-Out Package-on-Package), whi
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!AzhN!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Apple and NVIDIA do not compete for the same packaging lines today. Apple dominates AP3 (InFO), while NVIDIA fights for AP5/AP6 (CoWoS). However, as Apple moves
Fab 18 in Tainan is TSMC’s flagship facility for leading-edge production and it’s essentially “Apple’s fab.” The A19 Pro, A18 Pro, M3, M4 and M5 all come from F
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!EigO!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!1UnP!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Apple’s Taiwan concentration is the elephant in the room. Nearly all of Apple’s chip production, including every leading-edge A-series and M-series chip, happen
TSMC Arizona provides token diversification, but at current ramp rates, it won’t materially reduce Taiwan dependence until 2028+. Currently <5% of leading-edge

sections.title: Apple’s Five Transformational Chip Acquisitions

paragraphs.source_preview: Apple’s overarching semiconductor philosophy is simple but ruthless: control the “primary technologies” that differentiate the product. This journey, which bega
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!T7QL!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Ucm1!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!kX3T!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TRXa!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!qiO_!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!D3JM!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
1. P.A. Semi (2008, $278M): The Foundation
P.A. Semi was a boutique chip design firm founded by Dan Dobberpuhl, one of the most respected processor architects in the industry (creator of DEC Alpha, Stron
The team designed the A4, Apple’s first custom SoC. Jim Keller, who later designed AMD’s Zen architecture, led the A4/A5 development at Apple before departing.
Touch ID launched 13 months after the AuthenTec acquisition. AuthenTec’s architecture enabled the Secure Enclave, the hardware security subsystem that handles a
The Secure Enclave enabled Apple Pay (2014). A decade on, Apple Pay enabled $1.5T+ in transaction volume (2024). The $356M acquisition justified a financial ser
PrimeSense developed the 3D depth-sensing technology behind Microsoft’s Kinect. Apple bought them for $360M and spent four years miniaturizing the technology to
The TrueDepth camera projects 30,000 infrared dots onto your face, maps the 3D structure, and authenticates in milliseconds. Face ID is more secure than Touch I
The same technology enabled Animoji, Memoji, and became the foundation for LiDAR Scanner in the iPhone 12 Pro. 4. Intel Modem Business (2019, $1B): In-house 5G
Apple bought Intel’s smartphone modem business the same week they settled their lawsuit with Qualcomm. The timing was intentional. The settlement was a temporar
With Intel, Apple acquired 2,200 engineers (modem design, RF, validation), 17,000 wireless patents, Labs, equipment, IP across San Diego and Munich
The 5G modem was the final frontier for Apple and despite delays, the C1 modem shipped in iPhone 16e (2025), after five years of development. By 2027-2028, Appl
Apple has licensed GPU designs from Imagination Technologies since the original iPhone. In April 2017, Apple notified Imagination they would stop using their IP
Apple had secretly built an internal GPU team. The A11 (September 2017) shipped with Apple’s first custom GPU. The Apple GPU delivered 30% better performance th
Imagination nearly went bankrupt. They were sold to a Chinese-backed private equity firm. By 2020, both companies settled their disputes and entered into multi-
Global Design Operations
Apple operates 8,000+ chip engineers across 15+ design centers on four continents:
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!L_yD!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Israel is where Apple’s CPU performance leadership is designed. The Herzliya team, many recruited from Intel Israel, which created Pentium M, Core, and Sandy Br
The same country that gave Intel its best architectures now designs Apple chips that beat Intel.
The San Diego facility is squarely targeted at Qualcomm. Apple’s modem design operation sits literally down the street from Qualcomm’s headquarters. The office
DTCO: Design-Technology Co-Optimization
Apple co-defines the Process Design Kit (PDK) with TSMC. TSMC effectively dedicates hundreds of engineers to Apple, creating what is essentially a “virtual IDM”
The Relentless March of the A-Series and M-Series
The result of this manufacturing dominance is a performance lead that has compounded over a decade. Apple’s ability to move to the next node first allows it to
The transistor count growth is linear, but performance per watt is the real metric. The shift to N3E in the A18/M4 generation prioritized cost and yield over pu
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!jU4H!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!IUPy!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Ci1L!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Since 2013, Apple has consistently shipped industry-first features 12-24 months ahead of competitors.
Apple Silicon Technical Advantages
Apple’s performance leadership stems from architectural bets made a decade ago. While Intel and Qualcomm chased 5GHz+ clock speeds, Apple pursued ‘wide and slow
Front-End Architecture: Decode Width Parity
Apple introduced 8-wide decode with A14/M1 in 2020, four years ahead of competitors. But by 2025, the competitors caught up.
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!00WK!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Decode width is no longer Apple’s moat. The advantage has shifted to cache hierarchy, vertical integration and extremely efficient smaller E-cores.
Cache Hierarchy: Where Apple Still Leads
Apple’s philosophy: massive fast L1, large shared L2, and a System-Level Cache (SLC) before DRAM. The SLC allows CPU, GPU, and Neural Engine to share data witho
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!GAx7!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Apple’s SLC advantage is 3-4x larger than competitors with full CPU/GPU sharing. AMD’s Strix Halo matches on size but CPU cores cannot access it.
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!zTFs!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Apple and Qualcomm Oryon match on L1 size, latency and cache hierarchy, as the original Nuvia design team was largely poached from Apple. Intel’s L0/L1.5 tier s
Unified Memory Architecture
Traditional PC architectures have separate memory pools for CPU and GPU, requiring data copies. Apple Silicon uses Unified Memory Architecture: the GPU reads th
This eliminates the “copy penalty” critical for AI workloads. A 20GB LLM loads once. The Neural Engine and GPU access it simultaneously. Qualcomm mobile chips u
Vertical Integration
Apple’s efficiency edge comes from owning the less attractive silicon: custom Power Management ICs and storage controllers enable millisecond-level dynamic volt
The iPhone 17 Pro’s vapor chamber was co-designed with A19 Pro’s thermal envelope. Apple knows the exact sustained power budget (5-7W) and designs the chip acco
What’s changed in 2024-2025:
1. Decode parity: Intel, AMD, Qualcomm all reached 8-wide in 2024 2. SLC adoption: Qualcomm added 8MB SLC; Intel added 8MB memory-side cache 3. L1 parity: Qualc
Apple’s remaining advantages: larger SLC (32MB vs 8-10MB), true unified memory with full CPU/GPU coherency, and vertical integration enabling thermal co-design.
Next we’ll dive further into specifics: Apple wafer demand and economics at TSMC, including our forecasts through the end of the decade at the A14 node. We’ll q
And there’s more: packaging economics, what TSMC’s ex-Apple business looks like, Apple’s in-house efforts to replace Broadcom modems, competing efforts to copy
By Node, Chip, and Device; Apple’s Wafer Economics at TSMC
Apple’s wafer demand at TSMC grew 3.2x between 2014 and 2025, translating to an 11% CAGR. But that topline figure obscures the actual journey. The growth was a
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!fryo!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!64F1!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
From 2014 to 2017, demand grew steadily at around 10% annually as 16nm and 20nm ramped alongside iPhone volumes. Then growth stalled. Between 2018 and 2019, tot
The real inflection came in 2020. Demand surged 37% in a single year as N5 ramped and Apple Silicon launched for Mac. The following two years sustained that mom
Then came deceleration. In 2024, Apple posted its first year-over-year volume decline since 2013. N5 collapsed faster than N3 could absorb, and total demand con
The key insight is structural: Apple’s wafer demand moves in stair-steps, with multi-year plateaus followed by sudden jumps. Every jump aligns with a major node
Node Dominance Has Intensified
Apple’s share of each new node at launch tells a striking story. On 20nm in 2014, Apple drove 77% of node revenue at peak. On 16nm in 2016, the peak was 46%. On
That last number is extraordinary. Apple funded nearly the entire yield learning curve for TSMC’s most advanced node. By the time other customers reached volume
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!scr_!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!m4zH!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!GytB!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
TSMC Share Volatility
Apple’s share of total TSMC revenue moved within a band of 17% to 24% over the decade. Apple’s share spikes when it leads a new node ramp, then fades as other c
The 2023 peak of 24% coincided with N3’s launch year. The 2025 trough of 17% reflects AI accelerator customers absorbing leading-edge capacity. NVIDIA, AMD, and
If we remove the 2020 outlier from the CAGR calculation, the underlying growth rate drops from 11% to closer to 8%. That single year, driven by N5 ramp plus COV
N2: The Most Diversified Node Launch in TSMC History
N2 marks a structural shift in TSMC’s customer mix at node launch. We estimate Apple will once again the table with high share in N2 in 2026, but that represent
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!z0Rw!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic

sections.title: Apple’s Wafer Demand Model: Forecast Analysis (2026-2030)
sections.title_translation: 苹果晶圆需求模型：预测分析（2026-2030）

paragraphs.source_preview: N2 Breaks the Pattern
N2 enters production in 2025 with 234K wafers and scales rapidly to 778K by 2027. The volume trajectory looks familiar. But Apple’s share tells a different stor
At peak, Apple takes 48% of N2 revenue. That is down from 75% on N5 and 92% on N3. For the first time in a decade, Apple is not the dominant customer on the new
N3 Decays Fast
N3 peaks at 639K wafers in 2025, falls to 357K by 2027, and drops to 141K by 2030. That is a 78% decline in five years, faster than any prior node decay. Apple
N5 Gets a Second Life
N5 is the first Apple node to show a U-shaped recovery. It bottoms at 157K wafers in 2025, then climbs back to 434K by 2030. Prior nodes decayed monotonically;
The likely explanation is cost optimization. Apple is repurposing mature N5 capacity for lower-ASP products: entry-level Macs, older iPhone variants, modem and
1.4nm: Apple Dominance Returns
1.4nm enters in 2029 at 307K wafers and explodes to 808K by 2030. That exceeds N5’s all-time peak of 689K. The ramp is the steepest in the dataset, with year-on
Apple’s share of 1.4nm is projected at 67%. After ceding ground on N2, Apple reclaims node leadership on the next frontier. The pattern suggests Apple is front-
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!C_w6!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
New Chip Families: N-series and C-series
Two entirely new chip families appear in 2025 and together account for roughly a third of Apple’s incremental wafer demand through 2030.
N-series ramps 2.5x in its first year, jumping from 4.5K to 11.4K monthly wafers. Then it nearly flatlines for four years. The profile suggests a defined produc
C-series shows a different shape. It grows 6x from 2025 to 2028 (3.1K to 17.0K monthly), then plateaus. The curve is concave: growth decelerates each year. This
By 2030, N-series and C-series together represent 15% of Apple’s total monthly wafer demand. Neither existed in 2024.
Apple’s wafer demand at TSMC grew triple digits annually from 2008-2011 as iPhone and iPad scaled. Growth moderated to single digits by 2022 and turned negative
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!WQhG!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Y/Y Apple Spend
Apple’s annual foundry spend at TSMC grew from $2.3 billion in 2014 to nearly $75 billion by 2030, a 32x increase in 16 years. The only down year was 2023, when
Wafer Demand Volume Growth
Apple’s annual wafer demand at TSMC more than quadrupled between 2014 and 2030. Growth was not linear: it came in waves aligned with node transitions, with the
TSMC Share Recovery
Apple’s share of total TSMC revenue will likely recover as Apple transitions to higher priced A14 node in 2029, boosting its revenue contribution to TSMC.
The cycle appears consistent with prior patterns. Apple’s share spikes at node launches, fades as competitors scale, then spikes again when the next node arrive
A16 vs A14: The Node Optimization Shift
TSMC's A16 (N2) is architected for HPC workloads. Backside power delivery, gate-all-around transistors, and thermal envelopes are optimized for data center powe
A14 (1.4nm) rebalances the equation. TSMC is designing A14 for both mobile and HPC from the start, with the HPC-focused A14 SPR coming after the mobile-oriented
Consensus reads Apple’s N2 share decline as permanent displacement. The node roadmap says otherwise. A16 is Nvidia’s node. A14 is Apple’s comeback.
A-series: Maturity
A-series demand is linear. The 2014–2030 CAGR is under 2%, and there are stretches where absolute volumes are flat or declining. From 2017 to 2019, growth was z
The trend is real but plateauing. iPhone unit volumes have matured. Any demand increase reflects larger die sizes on newer nodes. By 2030, A-series falls below
M-series: The Growth Story
M-series shows exponential growth in its early years, with 2020–2023 CAGR exceeding 170%. But the curve is concave. Growth decelerates sharply after 2023, dropp
Even with that slowdown, M-series reaches 60% of A-series demand by 2030. The crossover is approaching. If M-series growth reaccelerates, perhaps through new ca
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!xnAA!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Portfolio Diversification
In 2014, A-series represented 92% of Apple’s wafer demand, representing a concentration risk.
By 2030, A-series is 29%, M-series is 16%, N-series plus C-series is 15%, and “Other” (PMICs, audio, display, wireless controllers) is 34%. No single family exc
Apple has evolved from a one-product silicon company to a diversified silicon portfolio. The concentration risk has halved over the decade.
Two new chip families (N-series and C-series) launch in 2025 and together account for roughly a third of Apple’s incremental wafer demand through 2030.
By 2030, N-series + C-series represent 15% of Apple’s monthly wafer demand. Neither existed five years prior.
Unit Decoupling
The decoupling between unit shipments and silicon value tells the real story of Apple’s foundry economics. A-series unit growth has essentially flatlined since
Diversification beyond iPhone
Apple’s wafer demand at TSMC has diversified meaningfully since 2018. iPhone accounted for 74% of Apple’s monthly wafer consumption that year. By 2025, that sha
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ocdJ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
The device base is massive but mature. Apple now ships over 600 million devices annually across all categories. But growth rates tell a different story: the 20%
iPhone volumes peaked nearly a decade ago and have oscillated in a narrow band ever since. The forecast through 2030 shows low-single-digit growth, barely keepi
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!hJUe!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!-vT0!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Wearables carried growth, then stalled. AirPods was the fastest-ramping product in Apple’s history, reaching meaningful scale within four years of launch. Watch
New categories are not moving the needle. Vision Pro shipments through 2030 remain a rounding error relative to the 600M+ device base. Its contribution is silic
Server appears in the forecast. A new “Server” line item emerges in 2024 and grows through 2030. Volumes are small, but the category’s existence signals Apple’s
The 2025-2030 growth outlook is muted. Total device shipment growth averages around 3% annually through the forecast, down from double-digits earlier in the dec

sections.title: AI Overtakes Apple at the Leading Edge
sections.title_translation: AI在先进制程上超越苹果

paragraphs.source_preview: For most of TSMC’s advanced node history, Apple was the undisputed volume leader. On N5, Apple’s quarterly wafer demand consistently exceeded the combined deman
The composition of AI demand makes the shift even more striking. NVIDIA alone is projected to consume more wafers on N3 by 4Q27 than Apple’s entire leading-edge
The structural implication is clear. Apple built TSMC’s leading-edge business over the past decade, funding yield learning and absorbing startup capacity on eve
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!CI6b!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
TSMC’s Platform Shift: HPC Overtakes Mobile
For most of TSMC’s history, smartphones were the anchor platform. From 2012 to 2021, smartphone revenue exceeded HPC revenue every single year, driven by Apple
This was dominance by design: Apple funded yield learning, absorbed startup capacity, and extracted the best economics before competitors could reach volume. Th
That era is ending.
But 2022 marked a structural inflection. HPC revenue surpassed smartphones for the first time and has accelerated since, growing at nearly 22% CAGR versus 11% f
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!IS-2!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
This shift has direct implications for Apple. Despite being the largest smartphone customer at TSMC, Apple’s leverage has diminished as HPC customers absorb dis
Apple is not standing still. The 1.4nm ramp in 2029 shows Apple reclaiming 67% node share, suggesting strategic capacity reservations made years in advance. Rum
The AI wave is too big to ignore, and Apple’s position at TSMC, while still formidable, is no longer uncontested.
Apple alone took >50% of 20nm, 16nm, N5, and of N3 at each node’s respective peak.
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!BczK!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
In 2022, HPC revenue surpassed smartphones at TSMC for the first time, and the gap has widened every quarter since. By 2025, HPC is projected to exceed $71 bill
NVIDIA alone now rivals Apple in leading-edge wafer consumption, and the broader AI ecosystem is growing at nearly 22% CAGR versus 11% for smartphones. Apple’s
Even as smartphone units saturate and HPC overtake smartphones, TSMC continues to increase its average revenue per smartphones, thanks to Apple’s relentless nod
One of the most misunderstood aspects of the Apple-TSMC relationship is the pricing model. Surface-level analysis assumes a fixed wafer price. The reality is a
The Known Good Die (KGD) Model
When TSMC launches a bleeding-edge node (e.g., N3 for the A17 Pro), yields can be as low as 55% during the initial ramp. A standard wafer pricing model ($20,000
The Deal: Apple negotiates to pay only for Known Good Dies (KGD) during the initial ramp. TSMC absorbs the cost of the defects. This effectively aligns incentiv
The Trade-off: Apple commits to enormous volumes (often buying 50-100% of initial capacity) that no other customer can match, effectively funding the R&D and de
The Transition: Once yields hit a mature threshold, the contract reverts to standard per-wafer pricing. This incentivizes TSMC to fix yields quickly.
Binning as Yield Optimization
Apple utilizes its M-series product stack to maximize yield. A defective M3 Max die with a broken GPU core doesn’t go in the trash; it becomes a binned M3 Pro o
A-Series: Less binning flexibility (iPhone volumes require uniformity), though the A18 vs A18 Pro distinction (5-core vs 6-core GPU) suggests aggressive binning
M-Series: Extreme binning. M3 Max exists in 30-core and 40-core GPU variants. This allows Apple to harvest dies that would otherwise yield losses. This strategy
In 2020, shortly after Apple commenced 5nm volume production for the iPhone 12 (A14 chip), Fab 18 reported having just 4 customers. This exclusivity explains ho
By 2024, as the node matured and yield rates stabilized, the number of customers at Fab 18 exploded to 45.
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vdcu!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!2aUo!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
This trajectory (4 to 14 to 21 to 45) shows the ‘Apple Wave’ thesis:
Phase 1 (Anchor): A new fab open with very few customers (Apple exclusive).
Phase 2 (Democratization): After Apple matures the node and captures the “Early Adopter Surplus,” the node opens to AMD, NVIDIA, and Qualcomm.
Phase 3 (“Platformization”): Apple effectively pays for the R&D of the industry’s leading edge while securing the first 12-18 months of exclusivity.
TSMC Capex Explosion (Driven by Apple)
TSMC’s capex trajectory reveals three distinct eras. Pre-Apple (2005-2009), annual spending averaged $2.4B. The foundry had no anchor tenant and limited visibil
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!rHve!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TC7Y!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Apple has effectively financed $73B of TSMC’s leading-edge capacity since the A8 launched on 20nm in 2014, representing 47% of all advanced node capex over that
These figures use lifetime utilization weighting, crediting Apple only for its share of each node’s output over a 5-year depreciation cycle rather than assignin
Qualcomm, AMD, NVIDIA, and MediaTek collectively account for 25% of TSMC revenue but bear roughly 30% of leading-edge capex burden because they backfill capacit
TSMC R&D Funding: Apple Role
TSMC’s R&D spending has grown 8x since 2010, from under $1B to $8B in 2025. But R&D intensity tells a different story. In the 2015-2019 period when Apple was th
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TYZW!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Apple’s Position Among TSMC’s Top Customers
TSMC’s customer concentration has steadily increased over the past two decades, with the top-10 customers growing from 44% of revenue in 2000 to 78% by 2025. Ap
That dynamic has now reversed. In 2024 and 2025, non-Apple revenue at TSMC grew at nearly double Apple’s rate, driven by AI accelerator demand from NVIDIA, Broa
Apple’s lead is shrinking. In 2020, Apple’s revenue was 4.6x the average of the other top-9 customers. By 2025, that ratio has compressed to 2.9x. Apple is stil
The 2022 dip is notable. Top-10 concentration actually fell from 74% in 2020 to 68% in 2022 before rebounding. This coincided with the crypto winter and memory
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ruoA!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TOEI!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!iCdz!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Ex-Apple top-10 growth inflected. From 2014-2021, the ex-Apple top-10 cohort grew at single-digit rates most years. In 2023, that flipped to +37%. In 2024, +40%
Concentration is a double-edged sword. With 78% of revenue coming from 10 customers, TSMC is increasingly dependent on a small number of buyers. Apple and NVIDI
Apple pioneered TSMC’s advanced packaging business. InFO launched with the A10 in 2016, and Apple scaled it from $1.8B in 2018 to $3.8B in 2025. CoWoS was a rou
Apple accounted for 78% of TSMC’s non-wafer revenue in 2020. That was peak dominance. InFO was scaling for A-series chips, and CoWoS had few customers. By 2024,
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!mCH9!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!JN0c!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic

sections.title: Apple Gross Margin Savings from In-House Chips
sections.title_translation: 自研芯片带来的毛利节省

paragraphs.source_preview: Intel Out, $4B+ In
The decision to abandon Intel for the Mac was the single most profitable supply chain decision in Apple’s history.
Prior to 2020, Apple paid Intel approximately $250 for a possibly overheating laptop CPU. Today, the M-series chip costs Apple estimated $40-50 to manufacture.
The Mac gross margin story is the clearest proof of Apple’s silicon thesis. In 2019, Mac GM sat at 28.5%, constrained by Intel’s $150+ processor ASPs and a road
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!e2JC!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!4H9L!,w_474,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!MQH3!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Intel’s failure to ship 10nm at competitive yields for over five years represented a multi-billion dollar transfer of value from Cupertino to Santa Clara. Apple
The Modem Bet: $6B to Break Qualcomm’s Stranglehold
The ongoing transition away from Qualcomm (estimated $1.2B/year savings) represents the final major piece of the puzzle.
The C1 modem represents Apple’s most expensive and most contentious insourcing bet. Qualcomm’s X75 5G chip commands a $20+ ASP with gross margins of 50%. Apple’
The real story is the NRE. Apple spent $6B (including the $1B Intel modem acquisition, 2,200 engineers, mask sets, and qualification costs) to get here. Break-e
Wi-Fi 7 In-House: $700M Recaptured from Broadcom
Apple’s move against Broadcom in Wi-Fi and Bluetooth receives less attention but carries equal significance. The N1 chip, Apple’s first in-house Wi-Fi 7 solutio
Gross Margin Savings
These savings recur and compound annually. Each A-series generation inherits prior design wins. Each M-series chip amortizes NRE across Mac, iPad, and now Visio
The Total Product Gross Margin trajectory tells the story: 34.4% in 2018 to 36.8% in 2025, with Mac leading the charge and iPhone following as custom silicon ma
More Apple in-house chips mean more business for TSMC. M-series boosted TSMC’s Apple revenue by $5B. The C-series and N-series wafers will incrementally add up
Huawei, Samsung, Xiaomi, ZTE, Google and others tried to replicate Apple’s success in vertical integration but failed to put together a coherent strategy. Huawe
Samsung with its Exynos apps processors and modems nearly replicated the A-series strategy but due to capacity and yield limitations still depends on external c

sections.title: Why Apple Will Not Build Fabs
sections.title_translation: 为什么苹果不会自建晶圆厂

paragraphs.source_preview: With $200 billion in cash, Apple could theoretically outspend any foundry on earth. Yet, the economics of building a leading-edge fab from scratch are prohibiti
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!hue8!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
The catch-up cost to replicate TSMC’s capability would require an annual capex of $50B+ (vs TSMC’s $30-35B) just to play catch-up. The density of human capital
For Apple, the return on invested capital (ROIC) of building a fab is negative. Using TSMC’s balance sheet as a service allows Apple to maintain 35-40% corporat
Every dollar Apple spends justifies $1.50 in TSMC capex, because TSMC knows Apple will fill those fabs.
The only scenario where Apple builds fabs is the one where Taiwan becomes inaccessible. At that point, the semiconductor industry has already collapsed. TSMC Ar
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!QySE!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Apple as the Engine of Global Semiconductor Capex; Apple as Industrial Kingmaker
Apple’s demand has catalyzed hundreds of billions in capital expenditure across the semiconductor ecosystem. The company ships over 600 million devices annually
The Companies Apple Built
Foxconn: Revenue grew from approximately $40B in 2006 to $208B in 2024 (TWD 6.86 trillion). The company now operates 230+ facilities across 24 countries with 90
ASML: Apple has never purchased directly from ASML, yet Apple may be the primary reason ASML exists in its current form. Apple’s leading-edge chip requirements
TSMC: Apple volumes consume the majority of TSMC’s leading-edge capacity. TSMC’s capital expenditure has increased fivefold since 2015, reaching $29.8B in 2024
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!8pFE!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!HgS2!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!AFJu!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic
Apple’s $3 trillion market cap has driven $300B+ in supplier capital expenditure over the past decade. Multiple companies count Apple as 40-75% of their revenue

sections.title: The Next Decade: 2025-2035; Faceoff with NVIDIA for Capacity
sections.title_translation: 下一个十年（2025-2035）：与英伟达的产能对决

paragraphs.source_preview: TSMC’s N2 node (2025-2026) marks a structural shift. For the first time, Apple faces genuine competition for leading-edge priority.
The shift in economics:
NVIDIA’s H100/H200/B100 chips sell for $25,000-40,000 each
Apple’s A18 Pro sells in iPhones for $1,099-1,199 (implied chip ASP: $100-150)
NVIDIA’s revenue per wafer: potentially 3-5x higher than Apple’s
TSMC has historically prioritized Apple as the anchor customer for new nodes. But AI margins are extraordinary. Will TSMC allocate N2 capacity to Apple’s predic
TSMC will thread the needle. Apple gets guaranteed allocation (contractual commitments from the lock-in era). NVIDIA gets incremental capacity from aggressive f
But the balance of power is shifting. NVIDIA will likely wield more power at TSMC and given Apple’s stagnant volumes it could be even more difficult.
We estimate AI accelerator wafers will overtake Apple at TSMC and will outgrow. While Apple will also participate in AI accelerator, it will come under pressure
Arizona wafer costs run 2.4x higher than Taiwan’s Fab 18. Labor is 3x more expensive. Foundation construction costs 4x as much. Raw materials run 2x. Utilities
Apple is already producing chips at Fab 21. TSMC lists Apple, AMD, Qualcomm, and NVIDIA as Phase 1 customers. Apple’s mobile SoCs, around 100mm² die size, are a
The node lag matters more than cost. Taiwan’s “N-2 principle” requires overseas fabs to trail domestic production by at least two generations. If TSMC is develo
Apple can absorb higher costs as a hedge against Taiwan risk. But Apple won’t shift majority production to Arizona unless forced. At 2.4x the cost of Taiwan waf
Currently 5% of leading-edge production is from Arizona. If this rises to 10-15% by 2028, it signals Apple is genuinely worried about Taiwan. If it stays at 5%,
TSMC’s A16 (N2) is architected for HPC workloads. Backside power delivery, gate-all-around transistors, and thermal envelopes are optimized for data center powe
A14 (1.4nm) rebalances the equation. TSMC is designing A14 for both mobile and HPC from the start. Power efficiency curves favor consumer devices again. Our mod
Markets read Apple’s declining N2 share as permanent loss. The node roadmap disagrees. While Nvidia wins A16, Apple reclaims A14 at a high share.
Meet with us at [PTC’26](https://www.ptc.org/ptc26/)!
As this year's PTC media partner, Dylan Patel, Daniel Nishball, and Jeremie Eliahou Ontiveros are looking forward to connecting with AI mega datacenters and the
We will also be spending time with telecom companies and equipment vendors to dive deep into exciting developments in scale-across networking.
Join us at our Happy Hour hosted on Sunday January 18! We'd love to connect with you.
